Looking good I think... I would like it if you could move a few things around to better align with the existing `Fsm`-related architecture of this package.

* Please move the `regex_to_nfa` function to be a method on the `Pattern` class as `Pattern.to_nfa`, a sibling of [`Pattern.to_fsm`](https://github.com/qntm/greenery/blob/7a8fff4473d3a2046f3a33a84f945ad0536565b1/greenery/rxelems.py#L623).
* Please move the `nfa_simulate` function to be a method on the `NFA` class as `NFA.accepts`, corresponding with our existing [`Fsm.accepts`](https://github.com/qntm/greenery/blob/7a8fff4473d3a2046f3a33a84f945ad0536565b1/greenery/fsm.py#L167C9-L167C16). As we go forward my hope is that the API of `NFA` will be almost identical to that of `Fsm`.
* Would it be possible to provide an implementation of `from_dfa` to correspond with [`from_fsm`](https://github.com/qntm/greenery/blob/7a8fff4473d3a2046f3a33a84f945ad0536565b1/greenery/rxelems.py#L269)?

I also see a linting failure in the CI run.

Sorry for the very long delay! I wanted to be sure to test this, and it took me a while to set up a test environment. We appreciate your patience.

Now that I've done that, I'm sorry to say that unless you provide some information that reveals that I made a mistake in testing,  **I don't support the changes in this PR**.

@Arka161 how exactly did you test these changes? Please be as specific as possible.

It's strange to me that image-building is failing for you on `master` (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/5369#issuecomment-1205738517) and succeeding on this PR, when I see the opposite. It's also surprising to me that the changes proposed in this PR are working for you.

* I'd expect that any patching of keys needs to happen *before* `apt-get update` and `apt-get install`, but you've placed it after those commands.
* with only the changes in this PR, I don't understand how the use of `conda activate` could present an error for me but not for you

Maybe the difference between how you tested this change and how I tested it (described below) will reveal what I'm missing.

### How I tested

I got a GPU-enabled EC2 instance on AWS with the following specifications.

- **AMI**: `Ubuntu 18.04 (ami-0b152cfd354c4c7a4)`
- **instance type**: `g4dn.xlarge` (1 NVIDIA T4 GPU)
- **region**: `us-west-2`
- **root volume size**: 100GB

Set up docker and CUDA drivers on it.

<details><summary>CUDA setup (click me)</summary>

```shell
# following https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/install-guide.html

# install docker
sudo apt-get update -y
sudo apt-get install -y \
    ca-certificates \
    curl \
    gnupg \
    lsb-release

curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg \
| sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg

echo \
  "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \
  $(lsb_release -cs) stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

sudo apt-get update -y
sudo apt-get install -y \
    docker-ce \
    docker-ce-cli \
    containerd.io

sudo systemctl enable docker
sudo systemctl start docker

# install nvidia-docker
distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID) \
      && curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg \
      && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/$distribution/libnvidia-container.list | \
            sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \
            sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID) \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo apt-key add - \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/$distribution/libnvidia-container.list | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID) \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/nvidia-docker/gpgkey | sudo apt-key add - \
    && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/nvidia-docker/$distribution/nvidia-docker.list | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

# get nvidia drivers and tools
# (versions selected by choosing the newest thing shown in `apt search nvidia-driver`)
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:graphics-drivers/ppa --yes
sudo apt-get update -y
sudo apt-get install -y \
    nvidia-driver-515 \
    nvidia-utils-515

# reboot the machine
sudo reboot
```

</details>

Then, set up `nvidia-docker`.

<details><summary>nvidia-docker setup (click me)</summary>

```shell
# back in, in a new session, check that drivers were set up correctly
nvidia-smi

# install nvidia-docker
sudo apt-get update -y
sudo systemctl stop docker
sudo apt-get install -y \
    nvidia-docker2
sudo systemctl restart docker

# check that nvidia-docker was set up correctly,
# and works for the base image relevant to this PR
sudo docker run \
    --rm \
    --gpus all \
    nvidia/cuda:8.0-cudnn5-devel \
    nvidia-smi
```

</details>

Next, created a small Python test script that trains a binary classification model using LightGBM's GPU support.

<details><summary>test-training.py (click me)</summary>

```shell
mkdir -p "${HOME}/repos/pr-5369"
cd "${HOME}/repos/pr-5369"

cat << EOF > ./test-training.py
import lightgbm as lgb
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)

dtrain = lgb.Dataset(
    data=X,
    label=y
)

model = lgb.train(
    params={
        "objective": "binary",
        "num_iterations": 10,
        "verbose": 1,
        "num_leaves": 8,
        "device": "gpu"
    },
    train_set=dtrain,
)
print("--- done training ---")
EOF
```

</details>

Next, I downloaded two versions of `dockerfile.gpu`...the latest one on `master` and the one from this pull request.

```shell
# Dockerfile from master
LGBM_COMMIT=c7102e56b246cc5cd73d9787b2c837c0bc384d1e
curl \
    https://raw.githubusercontent.com/microsoft/LightGBM/${LGBM_COMMIT}/docker/gpu/dockerfile.gpu \
> ./Dockerfile-upstream

# Dockerfile from this PR
curl \
    https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Arka161/LightGBM/master/docker/gpu/dockerfile.gpu \
> ./Dockerfile-pr
```

I built an image using the one on `master`, then ran the test script in it.

<details><summary>testing dockerfile.gpu from upstream (click me)</summary>

```shell
sudo docker build \
    -f ./Dockerfile-upstream \
    -t lightgbm-gpu:upstream \
    .

sudo docker run \
    --gpus all \
    --rm \
    --entrypoint="" \
    -v $(pwd):/LightGBM \
    --workdir /LightGBM \
    -it lightgbm-gpu:upstream \
    /bin/bash -c "source activate py3 && python test-training.py"
```

</details>

Here's what I observed.

1. image built successfully, with two warnings relevant to this PR:

> **Warning 1: GPG key**
>
> W: GPG error: https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/ubuntu1604/x86_64  InRelease: The following signatures couldn't be verified because the public key is not available: NO_PUBKEY A4B469963BF863CC
> W: The repository 'https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/ubuntu1604/x86_64  InRelease' is not signed.
>
> **Warning 2: source activate deprecated**
>
> DeprecationWarning: 'source deactivate' is deprecated. Use 'conda deactivate'.

2. training ran successfully, and used the GPU. Logs:

```text
/opt/miniforge/envs/py3/lib/python3.10/site-packages/lightgbm/engine.py:177: UserWarning: Found `num_iterations` in params. Will use it instead of argument
  _log_warning(f"Found `{alias}` in params. Will use it instead of argument")
[LightGBM] [Info] Number of positive: 357, number of negative: 212
[LightGBM] [Info] This is the GPU trainer!!
[LightGBM] [Info] Total Bins 5676
[LightGBM] [Info] Number of data points in the train set: 569, number of used features: 30
[LightGBM] [Info] Using GPU Device: Tesla T4, Vendor: NVIDIA Corporation
[LightGBM] [Info] Compiling OpenCL Kernel with 256 bins...
[LightGBM] [Info] GPU programs have been built
[LightGBM] [Info] Size of histogram bin entry: 8
[LightGBM] [Info] 30 dense feature groups (0.02 MB) transferred to GPU in 0.001001 secs. 0 sparse feature groups
[LightGBM] [Info] [binary:BoostFromScore]: pavg=0.627417 -> initscore=0.521150
[LightGBM] [Info] Start training from score 0.521150
--- done training ---
```

Then, I repeated this exercise using the Dockerfile from this PR.

<details><summary>testing dockerfile.gpu from this PR (click me)</summary>

```shell
sudo docker build \
    -f ./Dockerfile-pr \
    -t lightgbm-gpu:pr \
    .

sudo docker run \
    --gpus all \
    --rm \
    --entrypoint="" \
    -v $(pwd):/LightGBM \
    --workdir /LightGBM \
    -it lightgbm-gpu:pr \
    /bin/bash -c "source activate py3 && python test.py"
```

</details>

Here's what I observed.

1. The exact same warning about the NVIDIA GPG key is still raised.

> W: GPG error: https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/ubuntu1604/x86_64  InRelease: The following signatures couldn't be verified because the public key is not available: NO_PUBKEY A4B469963BF863CC
> W: The repository 'https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/ubuntu1604/x86_64  InRelease' is not signed.

2. A NEW warning is raised by the newly-added code.

> A deprecated public CUDA GPG key appears to be installed.
> To remove the key, run this command:
> sudo apt-key del 7fa2af80

3. The warning about `source` vs. `conda` is still raised.

> DeprecationWarning: 'source deactivate' is deprecated. Use 'conda deactivate'.

4. **Building the image fails**, with the following error.

> E: Conflicting values set for option Signed-By regarding source https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/ubuntu1604/x86_64/ /
> E: The list of sources could not be read.

If I revert all the `apt` related changes and just make the `source` to `conda` changes, **building this image still fails**.

> CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
> To initialize your shell, run...

Looks great! Super thorough testing, too. :O

Most comments are small, but one thing we need to look into a bit (regarding DatabaseMaxCounters).

One thing I wonder about, as we add things like this that are tightly coupled to the specific set of models we currently have, is how we could add a test that specifically catches cases where new models have been added, so we can make sure we update this code to handle them. One option I was thinking would be to just enumerate the set of syncable models and assert that they are an exact set. And then in that test's docstring, we provide pointers to the various places around the code that would need updating if a new model is added.

This snippet can be used to enumerate the current set of syncable models:
```
from morango.registry import syncable_models
all_models = syncable_models.get_models("facilitydata")
```

Like @komalali, I'm a bit nervous about the test changes because the main point of these tests is to have coverage of how [users would write unit tests using mocks](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/guides/testing/unit/) ([example](https://github.com/pulumi/examples/tree/master/testing-unit-py)). With these changes, the tests are no longer representative of what customers would write when writing their own tests using mocks. (Or do you think we need to rewrite our docs and example to use the `ImmediateExecutor`?)

However, CI flakiness is not good, so I'm OK with taking this change temporarily until we can address the problem for real. Part of that would be changing this back to the way it was. Which issue is tracking this? #7663?

The alternative is to disable these tests and have an issue open that tracks re-enabling the tests when the underlying issue is addressed, but then we'd have no coverage at all of this area in the meantime.

Some general comments about the approach:

I think, we need to follow closely the current workflow approach. For example, the same test run from `tests-cpu-kernels/test_cpuawkward_IndexedArray64_validity.py` clearly shows what are the input values for each of the 25 tests it would:
```python
def test_cpuawkward_IndexedArray64_validity_2():
    index = [1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]
    index = (ctypes.c_int64*len(index))(*index)
    length = 3
    lencontent = 2
    isoption = False
    funcC = getattr(lib, 'awkward_IndexedArray64_validity')
    ret_pass = funcC(index, length, lencontent, isoption)
    assert not ret_pass.str
```
The advantage of this is that it is clear which test values cause an error. For example, if I modify it to something non-valid:
```python
def test_cpuawkward_IndexedArray64_validity_1():
    index = [-1000, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]
    index = (ctypes.c_int64*len(index))(*index)
    length = 100000
    lencontent = -12
    isoption = True
    funcC = getattr(lib, 'awkward_IndexedArray64_validity')
    ret_pass = funcC(index, length, lencontent, isoption)
    assert not ret_pass.str
```

Sorry that I've been sitting on this change for a while, I had to find some time to mull it over.

This looks like a harmless change, but my problem with it is that, now, applying and unapplying a patch leaves the object in a different state than it was. consider this example:

```python
class Foobar:
    pass


def mypatch():
    return "foobar"


def main():
    obj = Foobar()

    try:
        obj.printer()
    except AttributeError:
        # expected
        pass

    with Patch(obj, "printer", mypatch):
        print(obj.printer())  # prints "foobar" as expected

    # this now raises a type error: TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable
    obj.printer()
```

i don't think this is clean behavior.

i see two possibilities:
* accept that Patch only works for properties that are already there (which is the way it works now, and I think is simple and predictable)
* if we must support attributes that don't exist, i suggest replacing `None` with a sentinel object, and when we unapply the patch and the old value is the sentinel object, instead of setting it to the old value, simply delete the attribute. this would then change the above code to raise the `AttributeError` again.

any thoughts?
Otherwise lgtm

I have done testing with AT x930 switch and it works properly by the following code:

```
import xlrd
import os
import time
import netmiko
from netmiko import ConnectHandler
import sys
 
def run_script(excel_file, script_file, print_list):
    workbook = xlrd.open_workbook(excel_file)
    sheet = workbook.sheet_by_index(0)
    for index in range(1, sheet.nrows):
        hostname = sheet.row(index)[0].value
        ip = sheet.row(index)[1].value
        username = sheet.row(index)[2].value
        password = sheet.row(index)[3].value
        device = {'device_type':"awplus_telnet", 'ip': ip, 'username': username, 'password': password}
        if print_list == "1":
            print("run script to", hostname)
            try:
               net_connect = ConnectHandler(**device)
               net_connect.send_config_from_file(script_file)
               print(" script  done for", hostname)
               net_connect.disconnect()
            except Exception as e:
             print(e)
        else:
            print ("|", hostname, "|",ip,"|", username,"|", password,"|")
            print(" ----------------------------")
            print(" Press OK to show the script !")
            input()
            print(script_file)
            print(" ----------------------------")
 
print(" #### welcome to Allied Telesis script automation tool ####") 
print(" #### ----------------------------------------- ####") 
 
excel_file = sys.argv[1]
script_file = sys.argv[2]
 
run_script(excel_file,script_file, "0")
 
print(" Press OK to run the script !")
 
input()
 
run_script(excel_file,script_file, "1")
```

1. I update method to getClosestStep to define it as: "find the closest step in this steps array" 
which means it can still returns null if it cannot find any closest step (only happens when steps is empty array)
return "closestStep ?? selectedStep" does not make sense to me to fulfill the definition

a.still guarantee we don't calculate closest step when it's not needed on range selection
b.but we waste time recalculate it if startStep is in steps (makes it the closest one)

Hi @parkerhancock thanks for the pull request! Please see my findings below:

I'm sorry but I think a lot of work remains to make this useful for LightGBM's users. Right now, this PR adds support for only a very limited interface, and with very little documentation. Even this PR's description doesn't really explain how this change improves LightGBM.

1. Why is this PR only adding support for reading in positions from a text file, and only for the case where the training data is also provided via a file?
     - I'd expect to be able to provide these positions the same way as other "metadata" like `initial_score` and `weight` are provided, and for how I provide it to not depend on how I provide the training dataset
2. Can you please add documentation ([here, maybe](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/9f78cceee4911dd56f4635dfd36d4482363db5aa/docs/Advanced-Topics.rst#L37)) covering this functionality? for example...
    - plain-language explanation of this functionality, the benefit of it, and when users should use it
        - (including links to relevant literature)
    - the fact that position bias factors can only be supplied via a file, only when training on a dataset also provided in a file,
    - the fact that that file must exactly be named `{name-of-train-data-file}.position`
    - the expected content of that file like:
        - dimensions (one line per group in `group`? one line per observation in the training data? something else?)
        - values (has to start with prefix `pos_`? what do different values mean?)
3. Does this implementation assume that all the positions are strings beginning with the prefix `"pos_*"`? (forgive me, I'm not very familiar with C++ so it's hard for me to tell)
    - if so... why is that preferable to providing integers? Integers would be much less expensive to store (both on disk and in-memory) and therefore less likely to cause memory issues for users.

#if PY_MAJOR_VERSION >= 3

Just skimmed the direct task transport class so far, but I can look more closely as you go through the current comments. My major comment is about keeping the control flow in the CoreWorkerDirectTaskSubmitter clear. One way to think about it is to make sure that we have certain invariants, and that the code to uphold those invariants is factored out into its own method. Like:

if there are tasks in the queue and non-full workers -> dispatch to a worker
if there are tasks in the queue and the workers are full -> request a new worker (same as before)
if there are no tasks in the queue stealable tasks, but no thieves -> request a new worker
if there are no tasks in the queue, stealable tasks, and a thief -> steal
else: return the worker

I think the code does match these invariants, but it's a bit hard to tell because it's hard to reason about where the parts on the right side are getting called.

After this PR, we should add a test that verifies that the undesired columns are not being read. That can be done with the `cache` option of array-reading functions (like [TTree.arrays](https://uproot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/uproot.behaviors.TTree.TTree.html#arrays)); see also [this tutorial](https://uproot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/basic.html#caching-and-memory-management). When you provide a dict as `cache`, it will be filled with unique string keys and array references as values. The unique strings include the TBranch name, along with a lot of other stuff. I definitely appreciate your point around this:
> I hope this MR can reconcile the two worlds: those who are dependent on `select/prefetch_related` and those who rely on custom `get_queryset`

It looks like overall this does the trick for that, and your approach of only using the custom resolver when `get_queryset` has been overridden seems fairly reasonable.

There are a couple changes in the PR that I have suggested. These are optional and do not affect the functionality of the code. Overall, I approve the merging of this pull request ✅ .

But, I am also adding the following so we know which direction to go for future improvements.

### Error handling: still not ideal

There is a still one thing about our error handling that bothers me. I can illustrate it with the following error message which was generated by raising a plain exception in the function body:

```
# >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> ERROR REPORT <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1787, in parse_known_args
        namespace, args = self._parse_known_args(args, namespace)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1996, in _parse_known_args
        stop_index = consume_positionals(start_index)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1952, in consume_positionals
        take_action(action, args)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1845, in take_action
        argument_values = self._get_values(action, argument_strings)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 2386, in _get_values
        self._check_value(action, value[0])
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 2433, in _check_value
        raise ArgumentError(action, msg % args)
    argparse.ArgumentError: argument command: invalid choice: 'string-art' (choose from 'clean', 'compare', 'config', 'create', 'info', 'init', 'install', 'list', 'package', 'remove', 'rename', 'run', 'search', 'uninstall', 'update', 'upgrade', 'notices')

    During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/exceptions.py", line 1133, in __call__
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/cli/main.py", line 63, in main_subshell
        args = p.parse_args(args)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1755, in parse_args
        args, argv = self.parse_known_args(args, namespace)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1794, in parse_known_args
        self.error(str(err))
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/cli/conda_argparse.py", line 197, in error
        sys.exit(subcommand.action(sys.argv[2:]))
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/string-art/string_art.py", line 26, in sub_command
        raise Exception("Hai")
    Exception: Hai
```

We're now bubbling up an exception, which is good, but because of the way that we use `argparse` we also get a weird looking exception from that code as well. This admittedly isn't that bad, but it could really confuse developers working on new plugins. They may choose to resolve the first error, only to later discover that this exception is in fact part of the functioning code.

### Potential improvements

During the first week of August as I was playing around with the new plugins code (this pull request), I really wanted to tackle this problem myself and actually wrote up an alternative implementation that addresses these issues:

https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11680

This implementation also enables support for CLI argument parsing libraries like `click` (see the example in the PR).

For future iterations, we should try to integrate some of the changes I suggested there.

Ok so, after some investigation, the issue that you see in the tests occurs because you've edited the `_retry_status` (and `_retry_async_status` in a way that now no longer performs the `test_ping` call. 

However the tests expect this call to be performed, and we test the mocked socket for presence of certain data, corresponding to us sending a [Ping packet](https://wiki.vg/Protocol#Ping_Request), which now doesn't happen, hence the failure.

When you'll be removing this additional data, make sure to also remove the read data the mocked socket holds, representing the response to our Ping packet (being the [Pong packet](https://wiki.vg/Protocol#Ping_Response)).

Hey @ashutoshkrris, thanks again for taking the time to clean up those commits and open a new PR. This is looking great!

Unfortunately, CI is failing because our linter, `flake8` is taking issues with some of the syntaxes. That's not your fault, our tests don't run `flake8` locally so you wouldn't have known. We need to document that better.

Do you think you could address the issues `flake8` is raising on CI? Namely:

```bash
imgix/urlbuilder.py:41:80: E501 line too long (87 > 79 characters)
tests/test_url_builder.py:30:80: E501 line too long (86 > 79 characters)
tests/test_url_builder.py:178:1: E302 expected 2 blank lines, found 1
tests/test_url_builder.py:178:80: E501 line too long (82 > 79 characters)
tests/test_url_builder.py:185:80: E501 line too long (85 > 79 characters)
tests/test_srcset.py:137:80: E501 line too long (90 > 79 characters)
tests/test_srcset.py:141:11: E275 missing whitespace after keyword
tests/test_srcset.py:145:80: E501 line too long (90 > 79 characters)
tests/test_srcset.py:149:11: E275 missing whitespace after keyword
```

One quick way to do this would be to use `black`, a python auto-formatter. In the root of your project fork, could do run something like:

```bash
# install black
pip install black
# run black on the files that have linting issues with max line-length set to 79
# ie, black --line-length 79 {source_file_or_directory}
black --line-length 79 tests/test_url_builder.py 
# make sure you have no more flake8 issues
tox -e flake8
```
